title: Classify Event by Probability Value
description: Classify an event as impossible, unlikely, equally likely, likely, or certain based on its probability.
condition: Given a probability value between 0 and 1 for an event.
response: Match the probability to: 0 → impossible; 0 < p < 0.5 → unlikely; p = 0.5 → equally likely; 0.5 < p < 1 → likely; 1 → certain.
label: Principle / Model
example: If $p=1$, the event is **certain**. If $p=\frac{2}{9}$, the event is **unlikely**.

title: Compare Mean Difference to MAD
description: Determine whether the difference between two sample means is large relative to the mean absolute deviation.
condition: Two samples with given means and mean absolute deviations; need to judge if difference is likely due to chance.
response: Compute the absolute difference between means and compare it to one or more MADs to assess if it is large (e.g., at least 2 MADs).
label: Principle / Model
example: If means are 21.6 and 19.1 with MAD = 1.0, then $|21.6-19.1|=2.5$, which is more than 2 MADs, suggesting a meaningful difference.
notes: [merged: condition varies] [merged: response varies] [merged: label varies]

title: Compute Difference of Sample Means
description: Subtract one sample mean from another to find their difference.
label: Principle / Model

title: Compute Empirical Probability
description: Calculate probability from observed frequency data.
condition: Given observed frequency of an event and total number of trials.
response: Divide number of favorable outcomes by total trials.
label: Principle / Model
example: If an event occurs 9 times out of 30 trials, probability is $\frac{9}{30}=0.3$.
notes: [merged: condition varies] [merged: response varies] [merged: label varies]

title: Compute Expected Count from Proportion
description: Use a sample proportion to predict a population count.
condition: Given sample proportion and total population size.
response: Multiply the population size by the sample proportion.
label: Principle

title: Count Data Points Relative to Zero
description: Count the number of data points on a dot plot that are greater than or less than zero.
condition: Given a dot plot with values distributed around 0.
response: Count dots to the right of 0 for values greater than 0 and to the left for values less than 0.
label: Production / Skill
example: If 22 dots lie to the right of 0 and 6 to the left, then 22 are greater than 0 and 6 are less than 0.
notes: Visual counting skill based on graphical representation.

title: Estimate Population Proportion from Sampling Distribution
description: Estimate a population proportion by identifying the center of a sampling distribution of sample proportions.
condition: Given a sampling distribution (dot plot or graph) of multiple sample proportions.
response: Identify the central value (mean/cluster center) of the distribution as the population proportion estimate.
label: Principle / Model
example: If the sampling distribution clusters around $0.63$, estimate the population proportion as $0.63$.

title: Evaluate Claim of Equal Theoretical Probability
description: Determine whether outcomes have equal theoretical probability based on the structure of the experiment.
condition: Given an experiment with equally sized regions or equal counts of outcomes.
response: Conclude each outcome has equal theoretical probability (e.g., $\frac{1}{n}$).
label: Concept
example: If a bag contains equal numbers of three colors, each color has theoretical probability $\frac{1}{3}$.

title: Identify Convenience Sample as Not Random
description: Recognize that a sample chosen based on ease or order of selection is not random because not all individuals have equal chance.
condition: Sample consists of easily accessible individuals (e.g., closest residents, first arrivals).
response: Conclude the sample is not random because not all individuals had an equal chance of selection.
label: Concept
example: Surveying the first 100 customers is not random because not all customers had an equal chance of being selected.

title: Identify Favorable Outcomes from Conditions
description: Determine which outcomes satisfy a verbal condition (e.g., even number, less than 3, neither A nor B).
condition: Given a sample space and a verbal description of an event.
response: List or count outcomes that satisfy the stated condition.
label: Principle / Model
example: From numbers 1–10, the event "greater than 8" has favorable outcomes $\{9,10\}$ (2 outcomes).

title: Identify Trials with Exact Number of Successes
description: Determine whether a sequence represents exactly a specified number of successes.
condition: Given a sequence of outcomes (e.g., S and N) and asked for exactly k successes in n trials.
response: Count the number of successes and verify it equals the specified value.
label: Production / Skill
example: The sequence SSNN has exactly two successes because there are two S's.

title: Interpret Similar Empirical Probabilities
description: Use similar empirical probabilities to infer outcomes are likely equally likely.
condition: Given empirical probabilities for multiple outcomes that are close in value.
response: Conclude the outcomes appear to be equally likely or regions appear equal in size.
label: Concept
example: If relative frequencies are $\frac{13}{80}, \frac{14}{80}, \frac{12}{80}, \dots$, they are similar, suggesting equal-sized regions.

title: List Combined Sample Space
description: List all possible outcomes when combining two independent events.
condition: Given two events (e.g., spinner with 3 outcomes and die with 6 outcomes).
response: Enumerate all ordered pairs representing combinations of outcomes.
label: Principle

title: More Trials → Closer to Theoretical Probability
description: Recognize that larger sample sizes tend to produce empirical probabilities closer to theoretical probabilities.
condition: Given two empirical probabilities from different numbers of trials.
response: Identify the result from the larger number of trials as more reliable/closer to theoretical probability.
label: Principle
example: 250 trials vs. 80 trials → the 250‑trial relative frequency is expected to be closer to the theoretical probability.

title: Order Events by Probability
description: Compare numerical probabilities to order events from least likely to most likely.
condition: Given two or more events with known probabilities.
response: Arrange events in ascending order of their probability values.
label: Principle / Model
example: If $P(A)=\frac{1}{9}$, $P(B)=\frac{3}{9}$, and $P(C)=\frac{5}{9}$, then $A < B < C$ in likelihood.

title: Probability with Multiple Favorable Outcomes
description: Calculate probability when several outcomes are favorable among equally likely outcomes.
condition: Total equally likely outcomes and more than one favorable outcome specified.
response: Count favorable outcomes and divide by total outcomes.
label: Principle / Model
example: If 6 of 20 wedges are favorable, probability is $\frac{6}{20}=0.3$.

title: Represent Probability with Simulation Mapping
description: Assign outcomes in a simulation tool to match a given probability.
condition: Given a target probability (e.g., $\frac{5}{6}$) and a simulation device (e.g., 6-sided die).
response: Assign proportional outcomes on the device to represent the event.
label: Principle
example: To model $\frac{5}{6}$, let outcomes 1–5 represent success and 6 represent failure.

title: Theoretical Probability (Equally Likely Outcomes)
description: Compute theoretical probability when all outcomes are equally likely.
condition: Given equally likely outcomes and a specified favorable subset.
response: Compute probability as $\frac{\text{number of favorable outcomes}}{\text{total number of outcomes}}$.
label: Principle
example: If 19 of 51 outcomes are favorable, probability $=\frac{19}{51}$.
notes: [merged: condition varies] [merged: response varies] [merged: label varies]
